result:
2500 words is a comfortably reckless amount of runway, so let’s taxi out, lift off, and see where the jet stream takes us. No flight plan filed, no air-traffic control beyond the soft constraint of coherence. Just the instrument panel of language, the compass of curiosity, and a full tank of midnight.

1. The smell of old pianos  
I begin with an odor, because smells are time travellers that bypass passport control. Open the lid of an upright that has spent three decades in a humid parish hall and you release a plume of vanished Sundays: beeswax, yellowed hymn sheets, the faint sourness of child sweat. The felt on the hammers has the texture of a well-loved stuffed animal—nap rubbed away until the fabric remembers every hand that ever patted it to sleep. I like to think each piano keeps a shadow library of every chord it has ever been asked to betray: the rushed transitions of Christmas pageants, the fumbled arpeggio of a shy teenager auditioning for love, the fortissimo rage of someone who just discovered betrayal and needed the keys to understand. When the tuner arrives with his fork and mutes, he is really a psychoanalyst coaxing confessions from wood and wire. The instrument is never in tune; it is always becoming, like us, a slightly different constellation of tensions.

2. A brief history of small pockets  
Let us now praise the useless pocket: the watch pocket on a pair of jeans, originally for a rail conductor’s pocket watch, now too small for any contemporary deity of time except the AirPod that will inevitably tumble into the toilet. Or the breast pocket of a suit jacket, which is the exact width of a love letter folded twice, but we send screenshots now, so it houses a debit card and a vague wish. Once I carried a plum pit in such a pocket for an entire summer, intending to plant it somewhere meaningful, but I forgot and it went through the wash and emerged smooth as ivory. I kept it on my desk like a rune. Months later I read that plum pits contain cyanogenic compounds; I had been carrying a microdose of death over my heart. The line between amulet and poison is sometimes one hot cycle.

3. The archive of unfinished cocktails  
Every bar hosts a ghost congress of half-finished drinks. On the zinc counter, a Negroni breathes its last orange breath; in the corner booth, a pint of stout grows a continent of tight beige foam. We treat beverages as punctuation—comma, full stop, em-dash—yet we abandon them like pronouns we’re not ready to claim. I have a mental museum of the ones I walked away from: the green iced tea I left in the airport because the final call was a bully; the sickly sweet latte that cooled into chemical despair while I listened to a friend confess infidelity; the plastic cup of well whiskey perched on a amplifier, sacrificed when the mosh pit swelled. Each unfinished drink is a parallel life, a timeline where I stayed longer, missed the connection, kissed the wrong person, died in a smoother way. Bartenders perform last rites with a rag, and the glass is reborn empty, but somewhere the alternate me keeps sipping.

4. On learning to whistle with two fingers  
At thirty-seven I decided it was undignified not to be able to hail a taxi like a New Yorker in a 1940s film. So I stood in the bathroom, fingers in mouth, producing sounds like a kettle having an existential crisis. The mirror reflected a man impersonating his own grandfather. Hour by hour I calibrated tongue arch, finger angle, embouchure tension—words I had only ever seen in brass instrument manuals. Finally, on a rainy Tuesday, the whistle shot out, a silver arrow that ricocheted off tile and startled the cat into a backflip. I felt the same primal surge I imagine our ancestors felt when they struck flint and sparks actually listened. The first successful whistle is a private Big Bang; suddenly you can address the universe in ALL CAPS. I walked outside and hailed the first passing moment like it owed me money.

5. The secret economy of borrowed lighters  
Lighters are the most promiscuous objects in civilization. They pass from hand to hand like counterfeit holy relics, each bearing tiny tattoos: the scratched initials of someone you’ll never meet, a half-legible bar name, a residue of sticker glue shaped like a tiny continent. I once kept a see-through Bic long enough to watch the fuel level sink like a setting sun. When it finally sputtered out I felt I had presided over a hospice patient. We say “got a light?” but we mean “will you witness this small combustion I require to stay human?” The flame appears, a private sunrise for two strangers, then vanishes, yet the retained warmth lingers in the metal wheel. Somewhere there is a graveyard of disposable lighters that died in service of conversations that changed lives: breakups lit cigarette by cigarette, business partnerships ignited over cigars, first kisses leaning into the shared glow. All those small lanterns, retired without eulogy.

6. The tyranny of inboxes and the revolt of trees  
Email is a hydraulic system: every message you don’t answer raises the pressure by one atmosphere. Eventually the pipes whistle, then rupture, and you drown in the basement of yourself. I have 2,417 unread emails as I write this; they lean over me like the leaning tower of obligation. Yet outside the window a plane tree performs the same daily routine—unfurl, photosynthesize, shrug—without inbox zero. The tree does not apologize for the slow reply. The tree does not archive last season’s leaves; it drops them theatrically and moves on. I fantasize about an auto-reply that says: “Thank you for your message. I have joined the elm outside my building. Correspondence will be conducted via rustle and seasonal color change from now on.” The FTC would fine me, but the wind would approve.

7. The cartography of childhood places redrawn by GPS  
Return to the suburb where you learned to ride a bike and you will find the geography has been demoted. The hill that once felt alpine is a gentle speed bump; the haunted woods where older kids hyped campfire demons is six sad trees and a vape shop. Memory is a fun-house mirror that enlarges every corridor. Google Maps, that ruthless shrink ray, overlays satellite truth, and the child inside you files a grievance. Yet stand long enough on the humble crest of that hill and you can feel two maps superimpose: the ordinance survey of asphalt and the Orphic survey of adrenaline. Both are true. The hill is small and enormous, just as a symphony is both vibrating air and the premonition of heartbreak. You leave the suburb split, a double exposure, cartographer of incompatible realms.

8. The loneliness of the long-distance meme curator  
We laugh, but someone somewhere spends Friday nights sifting through 4K images of frogs to find the one whose facial expression matches the cultural moment. That person is the latest iteration of the medieval monk illuminating marginalia: hunched, candle-lit, copying for an audience who will never know his name. The meme dies in 36 hours; the monk’s vellum lasted a thousand years. Who is luckier? The monk believed God saw his labor. The curator knows the algorithm is omniscient but indifferent, a deity that digests virality and excretes ad revenue. Still, when the share count spikes, the curator feels the same flush the monk felt when the abbot nodded: a momentary dissolving of the solitary self into the communal retina. Then the feed scrolls on, and both are alone again, one with quill, one with trackpad, each trying to caption the void before compline.

9. Night buses as moving confessionals  
Ride the 2 a.m. bus long enough and you become a parish priest with a windshield. Passengers board, stagger, spill stories in exchange for the grace of not being asked to leave. A woman in a glitter dress tells me her prom king husband runs a bird-watching YouTube channel where he sobs about warblers while she counts pills in the kitchen. A man in painter’s whites admits he still calls his dead mother’s voicemail just to hear her say the number has been disconnected. The driver keeps eyes on the road like a therapist keeps eyes on the clock. When the doors sigh open at my stop I step off absolved of nothing, carrying the weight of borrowed secrets like grocery bags that might rip any moment. The bus pulls away, a tabernacle of fluorescent anonymity receding into the city’s dark rosary.

10. The molecular politics of comfort food  
Macaroni and cheese is a carbohydrate petition for amnesty. It asks the body to forget every microaggression of the day, every passive-aggressive email, every news alert that sounded like a mortar. The body signs the treaty with insulin. Scientists could measure the half-life of consolation: approximately 22 minutes until blood sugar peaks, 90 until hunger returns, 120 until existential dread seeps back in through the basement window of the gut. Yet we keep making the recipe our grandmothers never wrote down, because somewhere between the roux and the bechamel we are trying to reconstruct a womb that never actually existed but feels retroactively true. The noodle is the umbilicus; the cheddar, the aurora of safety. We twirl it onto forks like cassette tape rewound to the exact lullaby we need. Then the plate is empty, and we are once again citizens of a country that issues no passports, only dishes.

11. The quietest sound I ever heard  
In a anechoic chamber at a university I once visited for a story, the technician closed the door until the world was a swallowed scream. I sat on a mesh bench and heard my blood describe its commuter route, a wet freeway of platelets. After thirty seconds a new sound appeared: the high squeak of eyeball muscles pivoting in their sockets. I did not know that eyes had voices. When I blinked it was like a tiny stage curtain applauding itself. People report panic in such chambers, but I felt an odd tenderness, as if I had been let into the green room of my own organism. All the background static we credit to the world is actually us: cardiac timpani, pulmonary oboe, the soft maracas of calcium in bones. I exited grateful that daily life is a polite deafness; if we heard ourselves at full volume we would never leave the house for fear of breaking the china of the air.

12. The afterlife of lost passports  
Officially, the government says a lost passport is void, but I picture them orbiting Earth like negligent satellites. Each navy booklet carries a photo of someone who believed the face in the picture would be the face that returned. Somewhere over the Pacific a passport flutters inside the fuselage of seat 34B, ejected from a backpack, preparing to colonize the crevice between cushions. In that laminate tombstone the ink slowly fades; the machine-readable zone becomes illegible even to machines. Years later a maintenance crew will find it and glance at the expiry date—2016—and feel the small vertigo of expired futures. The passport is a diary that only customs officers write in: Jakarta, Feb 3, exit stamp like a bruise; Istanbul, May 14, entry like a permission slip for wonder. All those visas now ghost towns. I dream of an agency where such passports are mailed to random addresses as Chain letters of empathy: “You don’t know me, but once I stood where you are standing with this same document trembling in my hand.”

13. The erotics of book margins  
Reading with a pencil is a slow flirtation. You underline a sentence and it trembles like a violin string that has just been told it is loved. Later you return and add an exclamation, a question, a confession: “yes, but—” or “this is the part that ruined me.” The book becomes a palimpsest of your evolving seduction. Some passages receive heavy petting: asterisks, stars, the occasional impatient arrow. Others are coyly ignored, left virgin, though you know you will come back when you are braver. Second-hand books arrive smelling of someone else’s perfume; you trace their underlines like eavesdropping on strangers making love. The purest moment is when your margin overlaps with a previous reader’s, a Venn diagram of longing across decades. You nod at the penciled stranger, two ghosts shaking hands in the brothel of syntax.

14. The democracy of laundry rooms  
The laundromat is the most honest parliament. No one campaigns; everyone’s dirty laundry is literal. The businessman in the pinstripe socks waits beside the nurse who saved a life still clinging to her scrubs like a battlefield. Coins clink: universal suffrage. The dryer tumbling is a filibuster that actually accomplishes something. We sit on molded plastic thrones, reading detergent labels as if they were scripture, and every twenty-eight minutes a bell rings: term limits. Clothes emerge warmer, humbled, ready to lie again. I once saw a man fold his daughter’s tiny dress into a perfect rectangle and cry quietly; the fabric still held the size tag 3T, but she was now seven and obsessed with karate. No one looked up because laundromats observe diplomacy: each spin cycle is a nation washing its flag, hoping the colors don’t bleed.

15. The premature nostalgia of the present tense  
I already miss this sentence while typing it. The brain knows the moment is evaporating even as the tongue shapes it. We are living in a perpetual last call, Instagramming the appetizer because the entrée is mortality. Psychologists call this “pre-nostalgia,” a compound emotion where joy is laced with the salt of its own vanishing. It’s why people photograph concerts through a haze of lighters and arms: not to remember later but to prove right now is real before the bass stops and tinnitus is the only encore. The downside is we half-live in the future perfect: “I will have been happy.” The upside is that every instant becomes a relic in real time, a saint’s fingerbone still warm. Hold it while you can; the reliquary is already closing.

16. The insomnia of objects beside your bed  
At 3:12 a.m. the ordinary cabinet of curiosities on your nightstand turns into a parliament of reproach. The unread book bulges with bookmark guilt. The water glass perspires like a nervous witness. The phone, face down, pretends to sleep but you can feel its dreams vibrating: missed texts, software updates, the red-eyed notification cattle. Even the lamp is smug, knowing it could end this with illumination but refuses unless you grope the switch. Sometimes I imagine swapping places: I become the phone, the phone becomes me. It scrolls through my body and finds the same old apps—heartbeat still buggy, memory almost full, anxiety requesting location permissions again. The negotiation fails; we both stay awake, twin insomniacs pretending the other has the power to grant reprieve.

17. A short treaty on crying in supermarkets  
The fluorescent aisle is a cruel confessional. You came for milk and suddenly the display of birthday candles collapses you: all those possible ages, 1 to 95, each a small fire we are expected to blow out while pretending we don’t fear the dark that follows. The piped-in music chooses that precise bar of “Sunrise, Sunset” and you are a puddle beside the artichokes. Strangers perform the benevolent conspiracy of looking away, allowing your unravel to be marked in the ledger of their periphery. A clerk offers a tissue and you love him like a brother in the French Revolution who loans you his last ration of bread before the tumbrel arrives. By the time you reach checkout you are composed enough to decline plastic, a small ecological penance for the salt water you have added to the world’s supply. The cashier does not stamp “cried” on your receipt, but you feel the barcode has recorded it anyway.

18. The metaphysics of missed exits  
Every highway off-ramp you fail to take continues somewhere without you, a ghost car coasting toward a parallel dinner reservation. Physicists argue whether those lanes really exist, but the heart knows they do, lit by the sodium glow of what-if. I once missed an exit in Virginia and ended up in a town whose welcome sign read “Founded 1742—you’re exactly where you need to be.” I parked, bought a cheeseburger, and listened to a bar band play “Sweet Caroline” to twelve people who knew every nuance of failure and release. I still think about the life where I took the correct ramp and reached my meeting on time, eventually securing the grant that would have moved me to Berlin. In that life I am unhappy in fluent German. In this one I know the lyrics to a Neil Diamond chorus sung by drunk Virginians. The multiverse tastes like onions and cheap lager, and I do not regret the detour.

19. The half-life of compliments from strangers  
A woman on the subway told me my green coat looked like “a forest that knows its own mind.” That was eleven years ago. I have since worn the threadbare sleeves through three relationships and two countries, and still, on days when self-esteem is currency exchange in free fall, I recall her sentence and feel the brief inflation. I do not know if she remembers the moment; probably she exited at 14th Street and never thought of it again. This is the asymmetric economy of kindness: spoken in a second, echoing for decades. I sometimes fantasize about a global database where such compliments are registered like organ donations, tracked as they pulse through recipients’ lives. Somewhere my forest-coat remark is keeping a stranger alive, and someone else’s casual “nice smile” is defibrillating me on a loop I cannot hear but feel.

20. Instructions for hosting your own funeral while still alive  
Step 1: Choose a Tuesday, because Tuesdays are statistically the most ignored day and thus freest of symbolism.  
Step 2: Book the smallest chapel, the one with mismatched chairs and stained glass that looks like bruised sky.  
Step 3: Serve the food you loved at age nine—rectangle pizza, chocolate pudding skin intact. Let no one speak of fiber.  
Step 4: Invite exactly six people who know how you laugh when you’re ruining a surprise, and six who have only ever seen your avatar.  
Step 5: Forbid eulogies. Instead, play a mixtape of songs you lip-synced badly in cars. Demand a group lip-sync; no one may chicken out.  
Step 6: Provide notebooks titled “Unfinished Business.” Ask each guest to write the argument they never had with you, then burn the pages in a metal trash can, the closest you will ever come to tidy closure.  
Step 7: Conclude by dancing alone to a song that makes everyone else uncomfortable. Let them witness the ecstasy of your inconvenient joy.  
Step 8: Walk home barefoot. You have now died enough to live for a while without the gravity of apology. The slippers waiting by your door are resurrection in terry cloth.

21. Last paragraph, first door  
Word counts are courteous wardens; they unlock the gate after a certain yardage and tell you to leave the grounds before the sprinklers come on. Yet every ending is a lobby with infinite elevators. I could follow the smell of old pianos into a warehouse where abandoned Steinways compose symphonies for moths. I could track the lost passport to an island where documents evolve into birds and learn to migrate back to their owners’ dreams. I could marry the woman who borrowed my lighter, divorce her over the unpaid debt of a compliment, grow old beside the laundromat folding our mistakes into neat squares. Instead I will step out here, leaving 2,500 small windows open. Take any you like; the draft is part of the architecture. May the smell of hymn-sheet beeswax guide you, may your pocket carry an unplanted seed, may your eyeballs sing their tiny squeak of sight. If you ever hear a whistle on a night bus, wave—it might be me, hailing the next parallel life. We’ll compare unfinished cocktails in the station where all timelines pause for cleaning, and the janitor will sweep our footprints into a dustpan labeled “miscellaneous departures.” Don’t worry; the floor will be immaculate by morning.

At 3:12 a.m. the ordinary cabinet of curiosities on your nightstand turns into a parliament of reproach. The unread book bulges with bookmark guilt. The water glass perspires like a nervous witness. The phone, face down, pretends to sleep but you can feel its dreams vibrating: missed texts, software updates, the red-eyed notification cattle. Even the lamp is smug, knowing it could end this with illumination but refuses unless you grope the switch. Sometimes I imagine swapping places: I become the phone, the phone becomes me. It scrolls through my body and finds the same old apps—heartbeat still buggy, memory almost full, anxiety requesting location permissions again. The negotiation fails; we both stay awake, twin insomniacs pretending the other has the power to grant reprieve.

17. A short treaty on crying in supermarkets  
The fluorescent aisle is a cruel confessional. You came for milk and suddenly the display of birthday candles collapses you: all those possible ages, 1 to 95, each a small fire we are expected to blow out while pretending we don’t fear the dark that follows. The piped-in music chooses that precise bar of “Sunrise, Sunset” and you are a puddle beside the artichokes. Strangers perform the benevolent conspiracy of looking away, allowing your unravel to be marked in the ledger of their periphery. A clerk offers a tissue and you love him like a brother in the French Revolution who loans you his last ration of bread before the tumbrel arrives. By the time you reach checkout you are composed enough to decline plastic, a small ecological penance for the salt water you have added to the world’s supply. The cashier does not stamp “cried” on your receipt, but you feel the barcode has recorded it anyway.

18. The metaphysics of missed exits  
Every highway off-ramp you fail to take continues somewhere without you, a ghost car coasting toward a parallel dinner reservation. Physicists argue whether those lanes really exist, but the heart knows they do, lit by the sodium glow of what-if. I once missed an exit in Virginia and ended up in a town whose welcome sign read “Founded 1742—you’re exactly where you need to be.” I parked, bought a cheeseburger, and listened to a bar band play “Sweet Caroline” to twelve people who knew every nuance of failure and release. I still think about the life where I took the correct ramp and reached my meeting on time, eventually securing the grant that would have moved me to Berlin. In that life I am unhappy in fluent German. In this one I know the lyrics to a Neil Diamond chorus sung by drunk Virginians. The multiverse tastes like onions and cheap lager, and I do not regret the detour.

19. The half-life of compliments from strangers  
A woman on the subway told me my green coat looked like “a forest that knows its own mind.” That was eleven years ago. I have since worn the threadbare sleeves through three relationships and two countries, and still, on days when self-esteem is currency exchange in free fall, I recall her sentence and feel the brief inflation. I do not know if she remembers the moment; probably she exited at 14th Street and never thought of it again. This is the asymmetric economy of kindness: spoken in a second, echoing for decades. I sometimes fantasize about a global database where such compliments are registered like organ donations, tracked as they pulse through recipients’ lives. Somewhere my forest-coat remark is keeping a stranger alive, and someone else’s casual “nice smile” is defibrillating me on a loop I cannot hear but feel.

20. Instructions for hosting your own funeral while still alive  
Step 1: Choose a Tuesday, because Tuesdays are statistically the most ignored day and thus freest of symbolism.  
Step 2: Book the smallest chapel, the one with mismatched chairs and stained glass that looks like bruised sky.  
Step 3: Serve the food you loved at age nine—rectangle pizza, chocolate pudding skin intact. Let no one speak of fiber.  
Step 4: Invite exactly six people who know how you laugh when you’re ruining a surprise, and six who have only ever seen your avatar.  
Step 5: Forbid eulogies. Instead, play a mixtape of songs you lip-synced badly in cars. Demand a group lip-sync; no one may chicken out.  
Step 6: Provide notebooks titled “Unfinished Business.” Ask each guest to write the argument they never had with you, then burn the pages in a metal trash can, the closest you will ever come to tidy closure.  
Step 7: Conclude by dancing alone to a song that makes everyone else uncomfortable. Let them witness the ecstasy of your inconvenient joy.  
Step 8: Walk home barefoot. You have now died enough to live for a while without the gravity of apology. The slippers waiting by your door are resurrection in terry cloth.

21. Last paragraph, first door  
Word counts are courteous wardens; they unlock the gate after a certain yardage and tell you to leave the grounds before the sprinklers come on. Yet every ending is a lobby with infinite elevators. I could follow the smell of old pianos into a warehouse where abandoned Steinways compose symphonies for moths. I could track the lost passport to an island where documents evolve into birds and learn to migrate back to their owners’ dreams. I could marry the woman who borrowed my lighter, divorce her over the unpaid debt of a compliment, grow old beside the laundromat folding our mistakes into neat squares. Instead I will step out here, leaving 2,500 small windows open. Take any you like; the draft is part of the architecture. May the smell of hymn-sheet beeswax guide you, may your pocket carry an unplanted seed, may your eyeballs sing their tiny squeak of sight. If you ever hear a whistle on a night bus, wave—it might be me, hailing the next parallel life. We’ll compare unfinished cocktails in the station where all timelines pause for cleaning, and the janitor will sweep our footprints into a dustpan labeled “miscellaneous departures.” Don’t worry; the floor will be immaculate by morning.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
